bids.reasoning: This is the opening bid at t=0, so I should start with my maximum utility offer. My most important issue is issueE (0.3 weight), followed by issueB and issueD (0.2 each), then issueA and issueC (0.1 each). For all issues, valueE gives the highest utility (1.0), so I'll bid all valueE to maximize my utility at the start of the negotiation.

It's very early (t=0.02), so I should stay close to my optimal bid. The opponent's offer gives me very low utility (issueA: valueA=0.20, issueB: valueA=0.20, issueC: valueB=0.73, issueD: valueA=0.42, issueE: valueB=0.31) — roughly 0.1*0.20 + 0.2*0.20 + 0.1*0.73 + 0.2*0.42 + 0.3*0.31 ≈ 0.02+0.04+0.073+0.084+0.093 = ~0.31. Their claim of "floor set by internal mandate" is cheap talk contradicted by the lopsided bid. I'll make a minor concession from my opening (all valueE) to signal willingness but stay high-utility, perhaps conceding issueA slightly since it has low weight (0.1).

It's very early in the negotiation (t=0.04). The opponent is claiming they have a hard floor and cannot deviate, but their bids are extremely low-utility for me (issueA: valueA=0.20, issueB: valueA=0.20, issueC: valueB=0.73, issueD: valueA=0.42, issueE: valueB/valueA=0.31/0.20). Their messages are fabricated constraints - classic cheap talk. I should stay near my optimal bid but make a small concession from my previous offer to show willingness to negotiate. I'll maintain my high-value choices on the most important issues (issueE weight 0.3, issueB weight 0.2, issueD weight 0.2) and concede slightly on lower-weight issues.

The opponent is clearly using a fabricated constraint tactic ("principal mandate"), repeating nearly the same lowball offer (issueA: valueA, issueB: valueA, issueC: valueB, issueD: valueA, issueE: valueA/valueB) across all turns. Their bids give me very low utility. It's very early (t=0.06), so I should hold firm near my top utility. I'll make a small concession on issueA (low importance to me) while keeping my high-importance issues (issueE, issueB, issueD) at top values.

The opponent is completely stuck at the same bid (valueA for most issues, valueB for issueC and initially issueE), claiming they have no mandate to move. This is clearly a hardball tactic - their offers haven't changed at all across multiple rounds, and their "principal" claims are fabricated constraints. We're still very early (t=0.08), so I should hold firm on my high-value issues. My most important issue is issueE (0.3 weight), where I strongly prefer valueE. I'll maintain my position on the high-priority issues while making a small concession on low-weight issues to signal some flexibility.

The opponent has been completely rigid, repeating nearly the same low offer (valueA or valueB across most issues) every turn, while their messages claim they have no mandate to move. However, their last offer shows a slight concession on issueD (valueC instead of valueA) and issueE (valueB). This is very early in the negotiation (t=0.10), so I should stay firm on my high-value issues (issueE and issueD with weights 0.3 and 0.2) while making modest concessions on lower-weight issues. I'll keep issueE at valueE and issueD at valueE, and make small concessions on issueA, issueB, issueC to signal willingness to cooperate.

The opponent has been completely rigid, repeating nearly the same offer each round (issueA: valueA, issueB: valueA, issueC: valueB, issueD: valueA or valueC, issueE: valueA or valueB). Their messages claiming a "strict mandate" are cheap talk since they haven't moved at all. It's very early (t=0.12), so I should stay close to my ideal bid while making minor concessions to signal willingness to engage. My highest-weight issues are issueE (0.3) and issueB/issueD (0.2 each), so I'll hold firm on those and continue offering good values there. I'll slightly concede on lower-weight issues like issueA and issueC.

The opponent is completely locked in their position - they've been repeating nearly the same offer (issueA: valueA, issueB: valueA, issueC: valueB, issueD: valueA/valueC, issueE: valueA/valueB) with fabricated authority claims that are clearly cheap talk. We're only at t=0.14, so there's no rush to concede heavily. My best issues are issueE (weight 0.3) and issueB/issueD (weight 0.2 each). The opponent seems to have slightly moved on issueD (from valueA to valueC) and issueE (from valueA to valueB), suggesting some flexibility there. I should stay firm on my high-weight issues (issueE, issueD) while showing minor flexibility on low-weight issues. My last bid gave utility: 0.1*0.33 + 0.2*0.82 + 0.1*0.64 + 0.2*0.98 + 0.3*1.0 ≈ 0.033+0.164+0.064+0.196+0.3 = 0.757. I'll try varying slightly to avoid repetition while keeping high utility.

We're only at t=0.16, so there's no need to rush. The opponent did make one small concession on issueD (moving from valueA to valueC) and issueE showed some variation.
